food_subgroup: Fresh cheese dish with vegetables and sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for burrata cheese (FOODON_03301710) but not for the full composed dish with grilled vegetables and basil pesto sauce. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for burrata with grilled vegetables and pesto. A00LC (fresh unripened cheese) is the closest defensible parent for the primary component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226760005 (Cheese) is the closest available parent concept; no specific burrata dish code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of burrata with grilled vegetables and basil pesto sauce, approximately 280 g, comprising one ball of burrata DOP (~125 g), a portion of mixed grilled vegetables and oyster mushrooms (~120 g), and basil pesto sauce (~35 g) drizzled with organic extra virgin olive oil.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Burrata cheese (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods) combined with component estimation for grilled vegetables and basil pesto
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for this composed Italian dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: burrata cheese (~45% by weight, USDA FDC NDB 1019 / similar fresh mozzarella-type data), grilled mixed vegetables including oyster mushrooms (~43% by weight, USDA FDC grilled vegetable composites), and basil pesto sauce (~12% by weight, USDA FDC pesto reference). Burrata is nutritionally similar to fresh mozzarella but higher in fat and cream content; values adjusted accordingly. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of dairy foods and mixed cheese-vegetable dishes — analogous estimate based on fresh mozzarella/burrata and low-GI grilled vegetables; University of Sydney GI Database reference for dairy and vegetable composites
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for burrata with grilled vegetables and pesto. GI estimated at 32 based on: (1) fresh mozzarella and similar soft cheeses have GI ~27 (University of Sydney GI Database, dairy category); (2) grilled non-starchy vegetables have GI ~15; (3) basil pesto (oil, nuts, cheese, herbs) has negligible glycemic impact. Weighted composite GI estimated at ~32, firmly in the low category (≤55). Glycemic load per 280 g serving = (32 × 4.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2.8 serving-weight factor) / 100 ≈ 4.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from burrata and pesto
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of the moderate sodium content (~588 mg per serving) from the cheese and pesto components, and consider limiting portion size or requesting reduced-salt preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~32) and low net carbohydrate content (~4.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low glycemic index (~32) and low carbohydrate load (~4.0 GL per serving), making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions; the high fat content from burrata and olive oil should be factored into overall daily energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from burrata cheese and pesto (~7.5 g saturated fat per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from burrata DOP and pesto is moderate (~21 g per serving); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and balance with unsaturated fat sources, noting that the organic extra virgin olive oil component provides beneficial monounsaturated fatty acids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Burrata with Grilled Vegetables and Basil Pesto Sauce is an Italian antipasto or starter dish featuring burrata DOP — a fresh Apulian cheese with a creamy, liquid stracciatella centre encased in a thin mozzarella shell — served alongside grilled mixed vegetables (typically zucchini, capsicum, eggplant) and oyster mushrooms, finished with a vibrant basil pesto sauce and organic extra virgin olive oil. The dish is vegetarian and gluten-free. Burrata DOP originates from Puglia, southern Italy, and is protected by Denominazione di Origine Protetta status. The texture contrast between the yielding, milky burrata and the smoky, charred vegetables is a defining characteristic. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~175 kcal/100 g), rich in calcium (~165 mg/100 g) and fat (~14.2 g/100 g, predominantly saturated from the cheese), and low in carbohydrates (~4.5 g/100 g), yielding a low glycemic index of approximately 32. The olive oil and pesto contribute monounsaturated fats and antioxidants. Clinically, the dish is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic load, but the saturated fat and sodium content warrant moderation for those with cardiovascular disease or hypertension. It is a good source of protein (~7.8 g/100 g) and calcium, relevant for bone health. In Singapore, this dish is served at Italian restaurants such as Ricciotti at The Riverwalk.
common_user_queries: Calories in burrata with grilled vegetables

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB, USDA FDC, ICMR-NIN) contains a direct entry for this composed Italian dish as served. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FDC data for burrata/fresh mozzarella, grilled vegetables, and basil pesto. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database dairy and vegetable references. Medium confidence is appropriate given the estimation methodology.
notes: 1. food_group corrected from input 'Grains and staples' to 'Dairy and eggs' as burrata cheese is the primary component and the dish has no grain/starch base. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA FDC component sources used for this estimation. 3. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates; a direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant dish would be required for high-confidence values. 4. Allergen note: pesto typically contains pine nuts (tree nuts) and Parmesan (milk); consumers with nut or dairy allergies should confirm with the restaurant. 5. The input cuisine_tags included 'Pasta', 'Pizza', and 'Chicken Dish' which are not applicable to this dish and have been excluded. 6. GI estimated at 32 (low category, ≤55) based on analogous University of Sydney GI Database dairy and non-starchy vegetable references. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FDC component data and carry medium confidence. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FDC component sources used for this dish estimation.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_group from 'Grains and staples' (input error) to 'Dairy and eggs' reflecting burrata as primary component.
Constructed full nutrient profile via USDA FDC weighted component estimation.
Assigned GI=32 (low) via University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for dairy and non-starchy vegetables.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg=-1 with explicit note as required by g3 sentinel value rule.
Verified aliases_by_language entries all appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Removed inapplicable cuisine_tags (Pasta, Pizza, Chicken Dish) from classification.
Added Chinese simplified and traditional names as supplementary multilingual coverage for Singapore market.
Confirmed all ontology match_type values use only allowed values: exact, closest_parent, none.
